content.text:
<user_query>
In @docs/development-plan-v1.md section 12.5, there is no manual testing until T11. Can't items be manually tested earlier?
</user_query>

Reading section 12.5 in the development plan to see how manual testing is sequenced.

[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]

Checking ticket prompts for manual verification before T11:

[REDACTED]

Here is how the plan actually reads, and how it fits together.

## Section 12.5 already has manual work before T11

The **0.5** manual matrix has **two** rows. The second is explicitly earlier:

```592:592:c:\Users\Ken Levy\OneDrive\Documents\Business\Ohana\Memories\docs\development-plan-v1.md
| **0.5**   | Authz isolation                          | T4,T10          | User A JWT cannot open User B client                                                              | 403 detail/list                                   | Manual / date |
```

So **authz isolation** is meant to be exercised and signed off once **T4** (authz middleware) and **T10** (web list/detail wiring) exist—not after the capture stepper.

**T10** also tells implementers to start informal manual coverage:

```415:415:c:\Users\Ken Levy\OneDrive\Documents\Business\Ohana\Memories\docs\development-plan-v1.md
**Verify:** Vitest/component tests where practical; manual smoke checklist started.
```

So the plan already allows (and nudges toward) **incremental** manual smoke before T11; it is not “no manual testing until T11” if you use both the matrix and the T10 verify line.

## Why the first 0.5 row starts at T11

The row **“Guided capture saves one memory”** lists **T11, T12, T14** because that scenario is the **full golden path** (photo → meta → record → save, plus airplane mode / resume). That path does not exist as a product flow until the **capture stepper (T11)** and the **offline/idempotency behavior (T12)** are in place; **T14** is when automated smoke is expected to lock the same path in CI.

You can still **manually** try pieces earlier (e.g. list/detail after **T9/T10**, signing or finalize behavior with tools or partial UI), but those are **not** spelled out as separate §12.5 sign-off rows today.

## If you want the doc to say that more loudly

Reasonable doc tweaks (only if you want them): add **extra §12.5 rows** for interim sign-offs—for example **list/detail/playback** after **T9–T10**, or **API-only JWT / 403** checks right after **T4**—so QA does not have to infer that from “Authz isolation” alone. Functionally, **yes, items can be manually tested earlier**; the matrix is a **release** checklist, not a prohibition on exploratory or story-level manual testing before T11.

[REDACTED]

input.new_string:
### 12.5 Manual test execution matrix

**How to use this matrix:** Rows are ordered **roughly** by dependency (API spine → signing → CRUD → web shell → capture → hardening). **Sign off** a row in the **Sign-off** column when its **Related tickets** are done and **Steps** pass in a suitable environment (local, staging, or ephemeral). **Stage 0.5** production still requires **every** **0.5** row below **and** the **T22** release checklist—not only the capture row. Rows without **T10**–**T12** may use **REST** or **curl** / API client plus seeded or synthetic JWTs; **T10**+ rows exercise the browser.


| Epic      | Scenario                                 | Related tickets | Steps                                                                                             | Expected result                                   | Sign-off      |
| --------- | ---------------------------------------- | --------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------- | ------------- |
| **0.5**   | Protected routes reject bad / missing JWT | T3             | Omit `Authorization` or send malformed token on a protected `**/api/v1/**` route; call `/health` without auth | Protected routes rejected per contract; `/health` 200 | Manual / date |
| **0.5**   | API `client_id` / memory authz (403)      | T4,T9          | Valid JWT; `GET` list or `GET` detail for another tenant’s `client_id` or unrelated `memory_id`    | **403**; error body has no PHI (**NFR-008**)       | Manual / date |
| **0.5**   | Image + audio **upload** sign URLs       | T5,T6          | `POST` sign endpoints; exercise oversize / disallowed MIME where safe                               | Policy enforced; URLs usable only for intended op | Manual / date |
| **0.5**   | Finalize idempotency + **0.5** media caps | T8            | Same **Idempotency-Key** twice; attempt finalize with >1 image or >1 audio                         | Same `memory_id` on replay; **400** on cap breach  | Manual / date |
| **0.5**   | List, detail, **PATCH**, soft **DELETE** | T9             | Cursor list, detail with media refs, allowed **PATCH**, **DELETE**                                | Contract + audit behavior per TDD; soft delete visible in list | Manual / date |
| **0.5**   | Playback **sign-read**                   | T7,T9          | `POST` sign-read for allowed `mediaId`; repeat for media outside caller’s scope                   | Time-limited read URL for allowed; **403** for denied | Manual / date |
| **0.5**   | Web list + detail + playback             | T10,T7,T9      | With test JWT: open list → detail → start playback (memory may be created via API or prior seed) | Playback works; empty/loading/error states sane  | Manual / date |
| **0.5**   | Web cross-client isolation               | T4,T10         | As user A, open user B’s `client_id` route (deep link or tampered segment)                        | **403** or safe blocked UX; no other tenant data   | Manual / date |
| **0.5**   | Ops smoke: `/health` + log spot-check     | T13            | `GET /health`; trigger sample traffic; inspect log fields                                         | **200**; structured logs; no PHI in payloads (**NFR-008**) | Manual / date |
| **0.5**   | Guided capture saves one memory          | T11,T12,T14     | Complete photo → meta → record → save; airplane mode mid-save → resume                            | Exactly one DB row; playback works                | Manual / date |
| **0.6**   | Multiple photos                          | T15             | Attach 3 images reorder                                                                           | Ordering persisted                                | Manual / date |

content.text:
Here is what changed.

### Updates in `docs/development-plan-v1.md`

1. **Document control** — Version **1.4 → 1.5**; **§13** revision history notes the §12.5 / §8 edits.

2. **§8 Testing and quality plan** — The manual-scenarios bullet now states that **0.5** §12.5 rows can be **signed off as tickets land**, while **Stage 0.5** still needs **all** **0.5** rows done.

3. **§12.5** — Added a short **“How to use this matrix”** intro (order, incremental sign-off, API vs browser, **T22** still required for prod).

4. **New 0.5 manual rows** (before the existing guided-capture row), each with **Related tickets**:
   - Protected routes / JWT (**T3**)
   - API `client_id` / memory **403** (**T4**, **T9**)
   - Image + audio **upload** sign URLs (**T5**, **T6**)
   - Finalize **idempotency** + **0.5** media caps (**T8**)
   - List, detail, **PATCH**, soft **DELETE** (**T9**)
   - Playback **sign-read** (**T7**, **T9**)
   - **Web** list → detail → playback (**T10**, **T7**, **T9**)
   - **Web** cross-client isolation (**T4**, **T10**) — same intent as the old “Authz isolation” row, scoped to the browser/deep link
   - **Ops** smoke: `/health` + log spot-check (**T13**)

5. **Kept** the end-to-end row **Guided capture saves one memory** (**T11**, **T12**, **T14**).

The former single **Authz isolation** row is split into **API `client_id` / memory authz** (sign off after **T9** with a REST client) and **Web cross-client isolation** (after **T10**). If you want a single combined row again for less paperwork, say so and we can merge them while keeping one API-first and one UI-first checklist in the steps column.

[REDACTED]

<user_query>
When a row has two tickets, e.g. the second row has T4, T9, do I test after T4, T9 or both?
</user_query>

Testing that row after T4 only: authz exists, but list/detail routes come from T9 — the steps require those endpoints, so the manual row isn’t runnable as written until T9 lands. Signing off: wait until both are merged (in practice T9 comes after T4 in the ticket map). Adding a one-line clarification to §12.5.

[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]

Rows without **T10**–**T12** may use **REST** or **curl** / API client plus seeded or synthetic JWTs; **T10**+ rows exercise the browser.
input.new_string: **How to use this matrix:** Rows are ordered **roughly** by dependency (API spine → signing → CRUD → web shell → capture → hardening). **Related tickets** names every ticket whose work the **Steps** assume; treat the row as **runnable and sign-offable only after all** of those tickets are merged (for example **T4,T9** needs list/detail routes from **T9**, not authz alone from **T4**). **Sign off** in the **Sign-off** column when **Steps** pass in a suitable environment (local, staging, or ephemeral). **Stage 0.5** production still requires **every** **0.5** row below **and** the **T22** release checklist—not only the capture row. Rows without **T10**–**T12** may use **REST** or **curl** / API client plus seeded or synthetic JWTs; **T10**+ rows exercise the browser.

input.old_string: | 1.5     | **§12.5** expanded **0.5** manual matrix (incremental API/web/ops rows + execution note); **§8** manual-scenario bullet aligned; **§12.5** intro clarifies sign-off vs stage gate.                                                          |
input.new_string: | 1.5     | **§12.5** expanded **0.5** manual matrix (incremental API/web/ops rows + execution note); **§8** manual-scenario bullet aligned; **§12.5** intro clarifies sign-off vs stage gate and **Related tickets** = **all** merged before a row is runnable/sign-offable. |

content.text:
**After both.** `Related tickets` is the full set of work the **Steps** depend on. You **sign off** that row only when every listed ticket is merged **and** those steps pass.

For **T4, T9** (API `client_id` / memory authz): **T4** adds the authz rules, but the steps use **`GET` list / `GET` detail**, which come from **T9**. So you cannot run that scenario as written until **T9** is in; there is nothing extra to “re-test only for T4” for that row beyond whatever automated tests T4 already has.

**T5, T6** is similar: both sign endpoints should exist before you sign off “image + audio upload sign URLs.”

**Exception in spirit:** You can **start** exploratory checks after the **first** ticket if it already exposes an endpoint (e.g. hit a route that exists only after T9). The matrix row is still **not** complete until the **last** ticket in the cell is done and the full **Steps** / **Expected result** hold.

This is now spelled out in the **§12.5** intro (with **T4,T9** as the example) and noted in the **1.5** revision line.

[REDACTED]

<user_query>
Can you give me step by step to manually test the first row in section 12. 
</user_query>

Checking the first §12.5 matrix row and how the API exposes auth and `/health`.

[REDACTED]

input.query: Where is /health route registered and which routes require JWT?

content.text: [REDACTED]
[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]

You almost certainly mean the **first row of the §12.5 manual matrix** (“Protected routes reject bad / missing JWT”), not the epic table in §12.4. Below is for **that** row (**T3**).

### Prereqs

1. **Run the API** with `JWT_ISSUER`, `JWT_AUDIENCE`, and `JWT_JWKS_URI` set (see `.env` / `.env.example`). If those are missing, the app fails at startup, so you cannot run this check until they are configured and JWKS is reachable.
2. Know the base URL (default from `.env.example`: **`http://localhost:3000`**).

### Steps

1. **Health without auth**  
   Send **`GET /health`** with **no** `Authorization` header.  
   **Expect:** HTTP **200** and a JSON body that matches the healthy response (e.g. `status: "ok"`, service name for the API).

2. **Protected route without auth**  
   Send **`GET /api/v1`** (or any real **`/api/v1/…`** route you have, e.g. image sign **`POST /api/v1/uploads/images/sign`** with an empty/minimal body) **without** an `Authorization` header.  
   **Expect:** HTTP **401** and an error JSON body whose **`code`** is along the lines of **`UNAUTHORIZED`** and message indicating a **Bearer** token is required (this matches the current hook in `apps/api/src/app.ts`: anything except `/health` goes through JWT unless you add more public routes later).

3. **Malformed / invalid Bearer token**  
   Repeat the same **`GET /api/v1`** (or **`POST /api/v1/uploads/images/sign`**) with:  
   `Authorization: Bearer not-a-real-jwt`  
   (or a structurally invalid token).  
   **Expect:** HTTP **401** and a body indicating the token is **invalid** (not a silent 200).

4. **Optional sanity check**  
   With a **valid** test JWT (same issuer/audience/JWKS as the API), **`GET /api/v1`** should return **200** and the small version payload, proving failures in steps 2–3 are auth-related, not a dead server.

### How to send the requests (pick one)

- **Browser:** open `http://localhost:3000/health` — should show JSON and **200**. Do **not** expect the browser alone to exercise step 2 on `/api/v1` without an extension that strips auth; use curl or a REST client for steps 2–3.
- **curl (Windows):**  
  `curl.exe -s -D - http://localhost:3000/health`  
  `curl.exe -s -D - http://localhost:3000/api/v1`  
  `curl.exe -s -D - http://localhost:3000/api/v1 -H "Authorization: Bearer invalid"`  
  Inspect the first line of the response for `HTTP/1.1 200` vs `401`.

5. **Sign-off**  
   Record date / initials in the matrix when steps 1–3 match **Expected result** for your environment.

If you meant the **first row of §12.4** (epic **E1–E6**), say so and we can turn that into a different checklist (that row is about epic review, not a single HTTP flow).

[REDACTED]

<user_query>
I want to send the requests with the browser
</user_query>

You can do almost all of this in the browser; only the **invalid Bearer** case needs one short step in DevTools because the address bar cannot send custom headers.

### 1. `/health` (no auth)

1. Start the API (same host/port you use in `.env`, often `http://localhost:3000`).
2. In the address bar go to: `http://localhost:3000/health`  
3. **Expect:** page shows JSON with a healthy `status` (and **200** — in DevTools → **Network**, click the request and confirm status **200**).

### 2. Protected route **without** `Authorization`

1. In the address bar go to: `http://localhost:3000/api/v1`  
2. **Expect:** **401** (not 200). Open **DevTools → Network**, select the `api/v1` request, open **Response** (or **Preview**) to see the JSON error body (`UNAUTHORIZED` / bearer required style message).

If the tab looks empty, the body is still there in the **Network** panel.

### 3. **Invalid** `Bearer` token (needs console)

The address bar never sends `Authorization: Bearer …`, so use the console **on the API origin**:

1. With DevTools open, stay on a tab whose URL is **`http://localhost:3000/...`** (e.g. the `/health` tab from step 1).
2. Open the **Console** and run:

```javascript
fetch("/api/v1", { headers: { Authorization: "Bearer not-a-real-jwt" } })
  .then((r) => r.json().then((j) => console.log("status", r.status, j)));
```

3. **Expect:** logged **`status` 401** and a JSON body indicating the token is **invalid**.

### Why not only the address bar?

- **`/health`** and **`/api/v1`** as normal navigations only send what the address bar allows (no custom auth header for the invalid-token case).
- **`fetch`** from `http://localhost:3000` to `/api/v1` is **same-origin**, so you avoid CORS issues you would get if you ran `fetch('http://localhost:3000/...')` from the Vite app on **5173** without the API allowing that origin.

If you want every step to be “click only” with no console, you would need a tiny HTML page on the API host or a browser extension that adds headers; the console `fetch` is the usual lightweight approach.

[REDACTED]

```bash
npm run dev
```

If the API exits on startup (and Vite stops too), set **`JWT_ISSUER`**, **`JWT_AUDIENCE`**, and **`JWT_JWKS_URI`** in `.env` so `@memories/api` can verify tokens.

content.text: [REDACTED]

Here is what was added.

### Root `npm run dev`

In `package.json`:

- **`dev`** — runs **`npm run dev:api`** and **`npm run dev:web`** in parallel with [**concurrently**](https://www.npmjs.com/package/concurrently) (`api` / `web` prefixes and colors).
- **`--kill-others-on-fail`** — if the API exits with a non-zero status (e.g. missing JWT env), Vite is stopped as well so you are not left with only the web app.

`concurrently` is a root **devDependency** (already installed; `package-lock.json` updated).

### Docs

- **`README.md`** — Quick start uses `npm run dev`; scripts table documents it; short note that **`JWT_ISSUER`**, **`JWT_AUDIENCE`**, and **`JWT_JWKS_URI`** must be set for the API to start.
- **`AGENTS.md`** — Commands list mentions `npm run dev` and the split scripts.

### How you run it

From the repo root:

```bash
npm run dev
```

You should see interleaved logs with `[api]` and `[web]` tags. With a valid `.env`, expect **API** on port **3000** (default) and **Vite** on **5173** (see terminal output).

`npm run dev:api` and `npm run dev:web` are unchanged if you only want one process.

content.text:
Here is what was done.

### Location

`cursor-template` is at:

`C:\Users\Ken Levy\OneDrive\Documents\Business\Ohana\cursor-template`

### Change in `cursor-template/AGENTS.md`

Under **Commands and validation**, a new bullet was added **after** install and **before** run-all-tests:

- **Run local development servers:** use a single root script (for example `npm run dev`) when this repo defines one; otherwise start each app using the scripts in the root **`README.md`** and **`package.json`** (for example separate dev tasks for the web app and API).

That matches the idea behind Memories’ line without naming `dev:api` / `dev:web` or `concurrently`, so it stays valid for other layouts (Turbo, one-app repos, different script names).

**Memories `AGENTS.md`** is unchanged so it can keep the concrete `npm run dev` / `dev:api` / `dev:web` commands for this monorepo.

When you copy `AGENTS.md` from the template into a new project, either keep this generic bullet or replace it with your repo’s exact script names, per `TEMPLATE_SYNC.md`.

Copy `.env.example` to `.env` and adjust (including **JWT_*** — the API will not start without them).

**Local JWT without your real IdP:** `.env.example` defaults match the built-in helper. In **one** terminal run:

```bash
npm run dev:local-auth
```

Leave it running. It listens on **127.0.0.1:3010** only, serves JWKS, and can mint a dev token at `http://127.0.0.1:3010/dev/token`. Then start the stack:

```bash
npm run dev
```

If the API still exits, confirm `.env` contains **`JWT_ISSUER`**, **`JWT_AUDIENCE`**, and **`JWT_JWKS_URI`** (the helper prints the exact lines). Production uses your platform issuer instead of this helper.

To run only one app (for example a second terminal is already running the other), use `npm run dev:api` or `npm run dev:web`.

content.text:
## What those terminal lines mean

The **`[api]`** block is **not** a random crash: **`buildApp()`** refuses to start until all three env vars are set:

```32:32:C:\Users\Ken Levy\.cursor\projects\c-Users-Ken-Levy-OneDrive-Documents-Business-Ohana-Memories\terminals\147440.txt
[api] Error: Missing JWT auth config. Set JWT_ISSUER, JWT_AUDIENCE, and JWT_JWKS_URI.
```

So the API never listens on port 3000. **`[web]`** still starts Vite because **`concurrently`** runs two separate processes; with **`--kill-others-on-fail`**, the web process should exit after the API fails (your log may be from a run **before** that flag was added, or the web process was still up briefly).

Root cause: **`.env`** had no JWT settings (they were only commented placeholders in **`.env.example`**).

## What we changed

1. **Clearer startup error** in `apps/api/src/app.ts` — the message now lists **only the variables that are missing** and points at **`.env.example`**, **`npm run dev:local-auth`**, and the **README**.

2. **`npm run dev:local-auth`** (repo root) — runs **`apps/api/scripts/local-auth-dev.ts`**, which on **`127.0.0.1:3010`** (override with **`LOCAL_AUTH_DEV_PORT`**) serves **`/.well-known/jwks.json`** and **`GET /dev/token`** with a short-lived dev JWT. It prints the exact **`JWT_*`** lines to paste into **`.env`**.

3. **`.env.example`** — default **`JWT_*`** values match that helper so a fresh copy works once the helper is running.

4. **README**, **AGENTS**, and **`docs/implementation-log.md`** — document the two-terminal flow.

## What you should do locally

1. Merge **JWT_*** from **`.env.example`** into **`.env`** if they are still missing.  
2. In **terminal A**: `npm run dev:local-auth` (leave it running).  
3. In **terminal B**: `npm run dev`.

If you already use a real IdP, set **`JWT_ISSUER`**, **`JWT_AUDIENCE`**, and **`JWT_JWKS_URI`** to that issuer instead; you do not need **`dev:local-auth`**.

**Tests:** `npm run test -w @memories/api` and `npm run typecheck -w @memories/api` both pass.

[REDACTED]
<user_query>Briefly inform the user about the task result and perform any follow-up actions (if needed).</user_query>

**Task result:** The background job **`Smoke-test npm run dev starts both processes`** (terminal **147440**) was **aborted** — it did not finish as a normal completed run (it was backgrounded after a timeout, then stopped).

**What it already showed:** With **no JWT env**, the **API** exited immediately with **`Missing JWT auth config`**; **Vite** still started until the process group ended.

**Follow-up:** Nothing else is required for that task. For a clean local run now: use **`npm run dev:local-auth`** in one terminal (with **`.env`** JWT lines matching **`.env.example`**), then **`npm run dev`** in another — as in the updated **README**. If an old **`node`**/Vite process is still listening on **5173** or **3000**, end it in Task Manager or `Stop-Process` and start again.

[REDACTED]

<user_query>

</user_query>

Checking the terminal output and how the API loads `.env`.

[REDACTED]

Tracing path resolution: `index.ts` is in `apps/api/src/`, so `../..` resolves to `apps/`, not the monorepo root — `.env` isn't loaded.

[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]

Fixing incorrect `.env` path: `apps/api/src/index.ts` used `../..` (resolves to `apps/`), not the repo root. Drizzle correctly uses `../../` from `apps/api/`.

[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]

## Entries

### 2026-04-30 - Local JWT dev helper and clearer API startup errors
input.new_string:
## Entries

### 2026-04-30 - Fix API `.env` path (monorepo root)

- **Area:** API
- **Summary:** **`apps/api/src/index.ts`** loaded **`.env`** from **`apps/`** (`../..` from `src/`) instead of the repo root, so **`JWT_*`** and other root vars were never applied when running **`npm run dev`** / **`dev:api`**. Resolved with **`../../..`** from **`src/`**.
- **Touched:** `apps/api/src/index.ts`, `docs/implementation-log.md`
- **Validation:** `npm run test -w @memories/api`

### 2026-04-30 - Local JWT dev helper and clearer API startup errors

content.text:
## Why it failed (your `.env` was fine)

`apps/api/src/index.ts` loaded **`.env` from the wrong directory**. It used `path.resolve(..., "../..")` from `apps/api/src/`, which resolves to **`Memories/apps/`**, not the repo root. So **`Memories/.env` was never read**, `JWT_*` stayed unset, and you still saw **Missing JWT auth config**.

`drizzle.config.ts` was already correct (`../../.env` from **`apps/api/`**).

## Fix applied

`../..` was changed to **`../../..`** so the path is the **monorepo root** (same place as your real `.env`). Tests pass.

Restart **`npm run dev`**. You should see the API start if **`JWT_JWKS_URI`** is reachable (run **`npm run dev:local-auth`** in another terminal if you use the default **`http://127.0.0.1:3010/...`** JWKS).

**Separate line in your log:** **`Port 5173 is in use`** means another Vite (or old **`npm run dev`**) is still on **5173**; the app moved to **5174**. Stop the extra process or keep using **5174** and align **`WEB_ORIGIN` / bookmarks** if you care about the port.

[REDACTED]

<user_query>
Ok. Back to the original goal, the checklist to manually test the first row in the table in section 12.5
</user_query>
[REDACTED]

Here is a **practical checklist** for the **first §12.5 row** (“Protected routes reject bad / missing JWT”, **T3**):

### Prereqs

1. **Repo root `.env`** has **`JWT_ISSUER`**, **`JWT_AUDIENCE`**, **`JWT_JWKS_URI`** (and the API loads it — the **`apps/api/src/index.ts`** monorepo-root `.env` fix is in place).
2. **JWKS reachable** from the machine running the API: for the defaults in **`.env.example`**, run **`npm run dev:local-auth`** in a **second** terminal and leave it running.
3. **API up**: **`npm run dev`** (or **`npm run dev:api`** only). Note the host/port (default **`http://127.0.0.1:3000`**).

### Checklist (map to matrix “Steps” / “Expected result”)

| # | Action | Expected |
|---|--------|----------|
| 1 | **`GET /health`** with **no** `Authorization` header (browser address bar or `curl.exe -s -D - http://127.0.0.1:3000/health`) | **HTTP 200**; JSON shows healthy status for the API service. |
| 2 | **`GET /api/v1`** with **no** `Authorization` header (browser address bar or curl) | **HTTP 401**; JSON **`code`** indicates unauthorized / bearer required (not 200). |
| 3 | **`GET /api/v1`** with **`Authorization: Bearer`** + a **non-JWT** or clearly **invalid** token | **HTTP 401**; JSON indicates **invalid** token (not 200). |

**Browser-only variant (same expectations):**

- **1:** Open `http://127.0.0.1:3000/health` → **200**, JSON body.  
- **2:** Open `http://127.0.0.1:3000/api/v1` → **401**; use **DevTools → Network** on that request if the tab does not show the JSON body.  
- **3:** On a tab whose URL is **`http://127.0.0.1:3000/...`**, **Console** → run:  
  `fetch("/api/v1", { headers: { Authorization: "Bearer not-a-real-jwt" } }).then(r => r.json().then(j => console.log(r.status, j)))`  
  → logged **`status` 401** and error JSON.

### Sign-off

When **1–3** match the table, record **date / initials** in the **Sign-off** column for that row.

Matrix reference:

```601:601:c:\Users\Ken Levy\OneDrive\Documents\Business\Ohana\Memories\docs\development-plan-v1.md
| **0.5**   | Protected routes reject bad / missing JWT | T3             | Omit `Authorization` or send malformed token on a protected `/api/v1/…` route; call `/health` without auth | Protected routes rejected per contract; `/health` 200 | Manual / date |
```

[REDACTED]

<user_query>
For the browser, item 2, how do I put the request in devtools?
</user_query>

For **item 2** you do **not** need to “build” the request in DevTools if you only care about **no** `Authorization` header.

### Simplest (no DevTools to send)

1. Open **`http://127.0.0.1:3000/health`** (or any `http://127.0.0.1:3000/...` page) so you’re on the **API origin**.
2. In the **address bar**, go to **`http://127.0.0.1:3000/api/v1`** and press Enter.

That is a normal **GET** with **no** `Authorization` header. Then use **DevTools → Network**, click the **`api/v1`** request, and check **Headers** (status **401**) and **Response** (JSON body).

---

### If you want to **send** it from DevTools (Console)

1. Stay on a tab whose URL starts with **`http://127.0.0.1:3000`** (e.g. `/health`).
2. Open **DevTools** (F12) → **Console**.
3. Paste and run:

```javascript
fetch("/api/v1").then(async (r) => {
  console.log("status", r.status);
  console.log("body", await r.json());
});
```

You should see **`status 401`** and the error JSON in the console.

---

**Note:** The **Network** panel is mainly for **watching** requests you already made (address bar, page load, or `fetch`). It does not give you a generic “send custom GET” form in Chrome the way some REST clients do; **Console `fetch`** is the usual way to fire a same-origin request from the browser.

[REDACTED]

<user_query>
I did the first two steps and don't see api/v1 in Network tab of Chrome inspect element
</user_query>

Chrome often **hides** that traffic depending on **when** DevTools was opened and which **filter** is active.

### 1. Turn on the right filter

At the top of **Network**, open the resource-type row and make sure **All** is selected (not only **Fetch/XHR**).

A normal visit to `http://127.0.0.1:3000/api/v1` in the address bar is a **document** navigation, not an XHR. With **Fetch/XHR** only, **`api/v1` will not appear**.

### 2. Open Network *before* you load the URL

1. Open DevTools (**F12**) → **Network**.
2. Optional: enable **Preserve log** (keeps rows when the page “navigates”).
3. Click **Clear** (circle with slash) so the list is empty.
4. In the **address bar**, go to `http://127.0.0.1:3000/api/v1` and press Enter.

You should get a row for that navigation—often under type **document**; the **Name** column may show **`v1`** or the path, not always the literal text `api/v1`. Click it and check **Headers** → **Request URL** and status **401**.

### 3. Easiest way to *always* see something named like `api/v1`

Use the **Console** on a tab whose URL is **`http://127.0.0.1:3000/...`**:

```javascript
fetch("/api/v1").then((r) => r.text().then((t) => console.log(r.status, t)));
```

That shows up as **Fetch/XHR** with a clear **`api/v1`** (or full URL) entry in **Network**.

### 4. Confirm you’re on the API host

The request must go to **port 3000** (the API), not **5173/5174** (Vite). If the address bar says `localhost:5173`, you won’t see the API’s `/api/v1` unless you use a full URL in `fetch`:

```javascript
fetch("http://127.0.0.1:3000/api/v1").then(/* ... */);
```

(from a Vite tab that may hit **CORS**; same-origin `fetch("/api/v1")` only works from a **3000** tab.)

[REDACTED]

<user_query>
I want to manually verify the second row for T4 in the table in section 12.5 of @docs/development-plan-v1.md 
</user_query>
[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]

Here is a **manual checklist** for the **second §12.5 row** (“API `client_id` / memory authz (403)”, **T4,T9**), focused on what **T4** enforces (JWT **must** be valid; path **must** match claims).

### Prerequisite (read this first)

That row lists **T4,T9** together: **`GET` list/detail** live under **T9**. In your **current** `apps/api` build, production routes do **not** yet include `GET /api/v1/clients/:clientId/memories` or `.../memories/:memoryId` (only tests inject those paths). If those `GET`s return **404** “Route not found”, **stop**: finish **T9** (or merge the branch that adds them), then run this checklist. Until then, **T4** behavior for `:clientId` is still covered by **`npm run test -w @memories/api`**, not by this manual row against the live server.

---

### Setup (any environment where the list/detail routes exist)

1. **API** running with valid **`JWT_*`** (e.g. **`npm run dev:local-auth`** + **`npm run dev`**).
2. A **valid Bearer JWT** whose **`client_id` / `client_ids` / `practice_id`** represent **User A** only (e.g. mint from **`http://127.0.0.1:3010/dev/token`** — default dev token uses **`client_id`** `8f9512d8-e88f-4f82-a8e9-6cb19a43ad52`** per `apps/api/scripts/local-auth-dev.ts`).
3. Pick a **different** UUID **`CLIENT_B`** that **does not** appear in that JWT’s client scope (any random UUID is fine, e.g. `f8aeed9c-97be-44eb-b13e-1a7d99b0dc0c`).
4. Optionally pick **`MEMORY_B`** for detail tests (another UUID; use a real id from another tenant if your API returns **403** for cross-tenant; **404** may appear instead for unknown ids depending on product rules).

---

### Manual steps (matrix “Steps” / “Expected result”)

| Step | Action | Pass criteria |
|------|--------|----------------|
| **A** | **`GET /api/v1/clients/{CLIENT_B}/memories`** (optional `?cursor=`) with **`Authorization: Bearer <User A token>`** | **HTTP 403**; JSON **`code`** is **`FORBIDDEN`** (or your contract’s denial code); **no PHI** in body (**NFR-008**). |
| **B** | **`GET /api/v1/clients/{CLIENT_B}/memories/{MEMORY_B}`** with the **same** User A token | **HTTP 403** (same checks as A). If the product returns **404** for unknown cross-tenant ids, record that and align with TDD/PM—matrix text assumes a clear **403** denial where the server chooses not to hide existence. |
| **C** | **Control:** **`GET /api/v1/clients/<token’s allowed client_id>/memories`** with the **same** token | **HTTP 200** (empty list ok) so A/B are authz failures, not a bad token. |

---

### Example (PowerShell, after routes exist)

Replace **`API`** if not `http://127.0.0.1:3000`.

```powershell
$API = "http://127.0.0.1:3000"
$TOKEN = (Invoke-RestMethod "$env:JWT_ISSUER".Replace("https://issuer.memories.localdev","http://127.0.0.1:3010") + "/dev/token" -ErrorAction Stop)
# Simpler: mint from fixed helper URL if that is your setup:
$TOKEN = (Invoke-RestMethod "http://127.0.0.1:3010/dev/token").access_token

$CLIENT_B = "f8aeed9c-97be-44eb-b13e-1a7d99b0dc0c"
curl.exe -s -D - "$API/api/v1/clients/$CLIENT_B/memories" -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN"
```

Fix the mint line — user should use literal:

```powershell
$TOKEN = (Invoke-RestMethod "http://127.0.0.1:3010/dev/token").access_token
```

Then inspect status line for **403** and response JSON for **no** names, transcripts, or other PHI.

---

### Sign-off

When **A**, **B**, and **C** match the table, enter **date / initials** in the **Sign-off** column for that row.

**T4-only angle:** **T4** is the middleware that binds `:clientId` / memory routes to the JWT; you are proving it by hitting real **T9** `GET`s. There is no separate “T4-only” manual row in §12.5 without those routes.

[REDACTED]

<user_query>
I just ran the first group in a terminal and got errors. Can you look and help?
</user_query>
[REDACTED]

Here is what went wrong in that terminal session.

### 1. First `Invoke-RestMethod` line (PowerShell parse error)

You had:

```powershell
Invoke-RestMethod "$env:JWT_ISSUER".Replace("https://issuer.memories.localdev","http://127.0.0.1:3010") + "/dev/token"
```

PowerShell treats the **`+ "/dev/token"`** as **extra arguments** to `Invoke-RestMethod`, not as string concatenation, which triggers *“A positional parameter cannot be found that accepts argument '+'.”*

**Fix:** build the URL in parentheses, then call `Invoke-RestMethod`:

```powershell
$mintUrl = ($env:JWT_ISSUER -replace "https://issuer.memories.localdev", "http://127.0.0.1:3010") + "/dev/token"
$TOKEN = (Invoke-RestMethod $mintUrl).access_token
```

Or skip `JWT_ISSUER` entirely and use the helper URL directly (simplest):

```powershell
$TOKEN = (Invoke-RestMethod "http://127.0.0.1:3010/dev/token").access_token
```

### 2. Second error: “Unable to connect to the remote server”

That means nothing is listening on **`http://127.0.0.1:3010`** — usually because **`npm run dev:local-auth`** is **not** running (or you changed **`LOCAL_AUTH_DEV_PORT`**).

**Fix:** In a **separate** terminal, from the repo root:

```powershell
npm run dev:local-auth
```

Leave it running, then run the mint line again.

### 3. `curl` → **401 “Bearer token is required”**

Because both mint calls failed, **`$TOKEN` was empty**, so the header was effectively `Authorization: Bearer ` with no token.

After **`dev:local-auth`** is up and mint succeeds, confirm:

```powershell
$TOKEN.Length   # should be > 0
```

Then:

```powershell
$API = "http://127.0.0.1:3000"
$CLIENT_B = "f8aeed9c-97be-44eb-b13e-1a7d99b0dc0c"
curl.exe -s -D - "$API/api/v1/clients/$CLIENT_B/memories" -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN"
```

**Note:** If that path returns **404** “Route not found”, list/detail routes from **T9** are not in this build yet; the authz row is meant for when those routes exist. With a valid token and a real route, a **wrong** `client_id` should be **403**, not **401**.

[REDACTED]

<user_query>
So what is the exact lines and sequence for me to paste in terminal to test, assuming the API is running on port 3000 in another termina? 
</user_query>

Use **PowerShell**. The API on **3000** is enough only for **calls**; you still need **`npm run dev:local-auth`** running in **another** terminal so **`http://127.0.0.1:3010/dev/token`** works (unless you paste your own Bearer token).

**Terminal A (already):** API → `http://127.0.0.1:3000`  
**Terminal B:** local auth helper → `npm run dev:local-auth`  
**Terminal C:** paste everything below.

```powershell
# Terminal B must be running: npm run dev:local-auth   (JWKS + mint on port 3010)

$API = "http://127.0.0.1:3000"

# Mint dev JWT (fails if nothing listens on 3010)
$TOKEN = (Invoke-RestMethod "http://127.0.0.1:3010/dev/token").access_token

# --- §12.5 first row: JWT / health ---
curl.exe -s -D - "$API/health"
curl.exe -s -D - "$API/api/v1"
curl.exe -s -D - "$API/api/v1" -H "Authorization: Bearer not-a-real-jwt"

# --- §12.5 second row: cross-client list (needs T9 route; otherwise expect 404, not 403) ---
$CLIENT_B = "f8aeed9c-97be-44eb-b13e-1a7d99b0dc0c"
curl.exe -s -D - "$API/api/v1/clients/$CLIENT_B/memories" -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN"
```

**What to expect**

- **`/health`:** **200**
- **`/api/v1`** without header:** **401**
- **`/api/v1`** with bad Bearer:** **401**
- **Last line:** **403** if list route exists and authz denies `CLIENT_B`; **404** if that route is not implemented yet (**T9**).

[REDACTED]

<user_query>
I have terminals A and B running as expected. I'm not sure how to understand the output of Terminal C. It looks like an error and not the correct results to me.
</user_query>
[REDACTED]

Nothing is wrong with that output — it just **runs four responses back‑to‑back** with **no blank lines** between them, so the closing `}` of one JSON runs straight into `HTTP/1.1` of the next (e.g. line 46: `..."memories-api"}HTTP/1.1`). That’s normal for `curl -s -D -`.

Here’s how to read it **in order**:

| Order | What you ran | First status line | Body snippet | Meaning |
|------|----------------|-------------------|--------------|--------|
| **1** | `GET /health` | `HTTP/1.1 200 OK` | `"status":"ok","service":"memories-api"` | **Good** — health OK without auth (§12.5 row 1). |
| **2** | `GET /api/v1` (no header) | `HTTP/1.1 401 Unauthorized` | `"Bearer token is required"` | **Good** — protected route rejects missing JWT. |
| **3** | `GET /api/v1` (bad Bearer) | `HTTP/1.1 401 Unauthorized` | `"Bearer token is invalid"` | **Good** — invalid token rejected. |
| **4** | `GET .../clients/{CLIENT_B}/memories` (real token) | `HTTP/1.1 403 Forbidden` | `"FORBIDDEN"` / `"Insufficient permissions"` | **Good** — valid JWT but **wrong `client_id`** → authz denial (§12.5 row 2). No PHI in the body. |

So you’re seeing **200 → 401 → 401 → 403**, which matches the checklist. **401** and **403** are **expected** for those steps; they’re only “errors” in the sense of HTTP status, not mistakes on your side.

**Tip:** To read one request at a time, run each `curl.exe` line **alone**, or add a separator:

```powershell
curl.exe -s -D - "$API/health"; Write-Host "`n---`n"
```

That keeps the next response from sticking to the previous JSON line.

[REDACTED]

<user_query>
How do I add a separator?
</user_query>

In **PowerShell**, run each `curl` and then print a line of dashes (or a blank line) so the next block does not run into the last JSON.

**Option A — after each `curl`**

```powershell
curl.exe -s -D - "$API/health"
Write-Host "`n----------`n"

curl.exe -s -D - "$API/api/v1"
Write-Host "`n----------`n"

curl.exe -s -D - "$API/api/v1" -H "Authorization: Bearer not-a-real-jwt"
Write-Host "`n----------`n"

curl.exe -s -D - "$API/api/v1/clients/$CLIENT_B/memories" -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN"
```

`` `n `` is a newline in PowerShell strings.

**Option B — tiny helper**

```powershell
function Sep { Write-Host "`n---`n" }

curl.exe -s -D - "$API/health"; Sep
curl.exe -s -D - "$API/api/v1"; Sep
# ...
```

**Option C — one line with semicolons (same as A)**

```powershell
curl.exe -s -D - "$API/health"; Write-Host "`n---`n"; curl.exe -s -D - "$API/api/v1"
```

You do not add a “separator” *inside* `curl`; you add `Write-Host` (or `Sep`) **between** commands so the terminal prints a visual break.
